questions.question: Approximate $\int_{1}^{5} \frac{1}{x^2} \, dx$ using a right Riemann sum with 4 subintervals.
Use a left Riemann sum with $n = 5$ to estimate $\int_{0}^{5} (3x - 2) \, dx$.

The table gives $f(x)$. Use a right Riemann sum with 3 subintervals to estimate $\int_{0}^{9} f(x) \, dx$.

$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c} x & 0 & 3 & 6 & 9 \\ \hline f(x) & 4 & 7 & 5 & 2 \end{array}$
Approximate $\int_{0}^{2} (x^3 - 3x) \, dx$ using a midpoint Riemann sum with 4 subintervals.
The rate of change of a population (thousands per year) is $r(t) = 0.5t^2$. Use a right Riemann sum with 4 subintervals to estimate the total change from $t = 0$ to $t = 4$.
For $f(x) = \ln x$ on $[1,4]$, use a left Riemann sum with 6 subintervals.

Given the table, use a left Riemann sum to approximate $\int_{0}^{10} g(x) \, dx$.

$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c} x & 0 & 2 & 4 & 7 & 10 \\ \hline g(x) & 1 & 3 & 5 & 2 & 4 \end{array}$
questions.explanation: Subintervals have unequal widths: [0,2], [2,4], [4,7], [7,10] with widths 2, 2, 3, 3. Left endpoints: 0, 2, 4, 7. Multiply by widths: $1\times2=2$, $3\times2=6$, $5\times3=15$, $2\times3=6$. Sum = 29.

questions.question: Approximate $\int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos^2 x \, dx$ using a right Riemann sum with $n = 4$.

A car's velocity (mph) is recorded every 2 hours: $t=0:40, 2:50, 4:45, 6:55, 8:60$. Use a midpoint sum with 4 subintervals to estimate the distance traveled from $t = 0$ to $t = 8$. (Assume linear interpolation for midpoints.)
questions.explanation: $\Delta t = 2$; midpoints: $t = 1, 3, 5, 7$. Interpolated velocities: 45, 47.5, 50, 57.5. Sum = 200, times 2 = 400 miles.

questions.question: Which of the following represents a midpoint Riemann sum with $n = 6$ to approximate $\int_{0}^{3} xe^{-x} \, dx$?

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered Riemann sum approximations.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! You understand the basics of Riemann sums. Review midpoint calculations.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on identifying endpoints and calculating $\Delta x$ correctly.
scoreMessages.0: Review the definition of Riemann sums: $\sum f(x_i^*) \Delta x$.
